content: Blockchain records transactions without a central bank by using a network of many computers, each holding a copy of the transaction history. Instead of one trusted middleman, these computers work together to verify new transactions by solving complex puzzles, a process known as mining. This ensures that no one can change past transactions without the whole network noticing. Since the record is duplicated worldwide, altering just one copy won’t work if the others don’t match. To successfully cheat, someone would need to control most of the computers at once, which is extremely difficult and expensive. Trust in blockchain comes from math and the network itself, not from any single institution. Many people and companies already use blockchain to securely track transactions, proving it works beyond theory. In short, blockchain’s strength lies in spreading the record across many computers and using complex checks to keep the system honest without relying on a central bank.
